examples.text: لا تَحْسَبَنَّ الَّذِينَ يَفْرَحُونَ بِمَا أَتَوْا وَيُحِبُّونَ أَنْ يُحْمَدُوا بِمَا لَمْ يَفْعَلُوا فَلَا تَحْسَبَنَّهُمْ بِمَفَازَةٍ مِنَ الْعَذَابِ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ

senses.gloss: place of victory, of flight, of destruction

etymology.text: Noun of place from the verb فَازَ (fāza, “to escape, be victorious, perish”), from the root ف و ز (f w z).

body.text: (S, A, O, K.) So in the Kur [iii. 185],
Do thou by no means reckon them
in a place of security from punishment
]: (S, A, * O:) or, accord. to Fr, the meaning here is,
of the attainment or acquisition of that which one desires or seeks,
of that whereby one becomes in a happy or good state;
state of temporary safety which is between the present life and that which is to come.
a desert in which is no water:
a desert in which is no water for the space of a journey of two nights or more:
when there is none for the space of a journey of a night and a day, it is not thus called. (ISh, O, TA:) or
a tract in which two wateringplaces are so far apart that camels are kept from drinking two days, with a portion of the day preceding them and of the day following them,
[accord. to that which is generally preferred of the explanations of the term
such as is between that in which camels are kept from drinking two days &c. as above, and that in which other animals drink on alternate days;
: (TA:) so called to prognosticate good fortune, and safety. (As, IF, S, A, O, Msb,) as meaning a place of safety, (A,) from
signifying “ he became safe: ” (Msb:) or from

. (AHei, TA.) signifying “ he perished, ” (IAar, S, O, * AHei,) or “ he died: ” (Msb, TA:) AHei condemns the former of these assertions; but Az and ISd say that it is the more commonly approved, though the latter is the more agreeable with analogy: (TA:) or it is so called because he who comes forth from it, having traversed it, is safe: (IAar, TA:) the pl. is
. so in a trad. of Kaab Ibn-Málik;
looked forward to, a far journey and a desert,
